textPlain: Interesting stuff! I knew about lvalues and rvalues but I never knew about concepts like "glvalue" or "prvalue" or "xvalue" that the linked page talks about.It makes sense that C++ avoids unnecessary copying or object creation whenever possible, that's pretty much C++'s M.O.

I tried to read this but am stuck on his language. He uses the phrase "move from" repeatedly without explaining it. My best guess is he is not a native English speaker and just means "move" in the sense of relocate the memory storage location of an object?

"Move" in the sense of
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/3106110/what-is-move-sem...Now, if you don't know what "move semantics" is, then "lvalues can't be moved from" isn't terribly helpful, and if you do then it's tautological, so I'm not saying you're wrong to criticize. :) But in a C++ context, "move" does have a single specific meaning — the one he's using properly if opaquely-to-non-C++ers.
"move from" means to pass into an r-value reference function parameter, for instance a move constructor, move assignment operator, or forwarding reference.
